title: Recency-Weighted Relevance
concept: Blending topical relevance with recency so stale information is naturally deprioritized.

exercises.prompt: Quick recap: why did the previous lesson warn against filling the context window with five near-identical high-scoring chunks?

options.text: Because they add little new information, wasting space that could hold diverse, useful content
Because identical chunks always contain factual errors
Because the model refuses to read repeated text

exercises.explain: Diversity-aware selection avoids redundant near-duplicate chunks so the limited context window is spent on varied, genuinely useful information instead of five copies of the same idea.

cards.title: Yesterday's news isn't news
cards.body: Imagine asking an assistant 'Who won the game last night?' and it confidently quotes a highlight reel from three years ago because the wording matches perfectly. Topic match alone isn't enough — time matters too.

cards.title: What is Recency-Weighted Relevance?
cards.body: It's a scoring trick where a chunk's final score = how topically relevant it is, adjusted by how recent it is. Fresh, on-topic info gets boosted; old, on-topic info gets gently pushed down, so stale facts don't dominate the context window.

cards.title: Bread at the bakery
cards.body: A fresh loaf from this morning beats a stale loaf from last week, even if the stale one still 'matches' the recipe. Recency-weighted relevance treats old info like day-old bread: still usable, but ranked lower than the fresh batch.

cards.title: Stock price question
cards.body: User asks 'What's the current stock price of Acme?' A chunk from today saying '$52' should beat a chunk from 6 months ago saying '$41', even if the old chunk uses fancier matching keywords. Recency weighting makes today's number win.

cards.title: Myth: 'Newest is always best'
cards.body: Not true! A brand-new but off-topic document shouldn't beat an older, spot-on match. Recency is a WEIGHT added to relevance, not a replacement for it — both signals combine into one score.

cards.title: Relevance alone vs. Relevance + Recency
cards.body: Adding a time-decay factor changes which chunk wins when content ages.

left.text: Old detailed doc outranks a shorter but fresher one, even though the old facts changed

right.text: Score = topic match reduced by age penalty, so fresher on-topic doc rises to the top

exercises.prompt: Which scenario best demonstrates recency-weighted relevance working correctly?

options.text: A 2-year-old FAQ about 'current pricing' outranks a pricing page updated yesterday
A yesterday-updated pricing page outranks a 2-year-old FAQ that used the same keyword 'pricing'
All documents are sorted only by upload date, ignoring what they're about
Two equally old, equally relevant documents are ranked randomly

exercises.explain: Recency-weighted relevance boosts the fresher, on-topic document over an older one covering the same topic, since the fresher content is more likely to reflect current truth.

exercises.prompt: True or False: In recency-weighted relevance, a document's age should completely override its topical relevance score.
exercises.explain: Recency is meant to be blended with relevance, not replace it. A perfectly on-topic older document can still beat a brand-new but irrelevant one; recency just nudges the ranking, it doesn't erase topical matching.

exercises.sentence: Recency-weighted relevance combines topical match with a time-based decay so stale information is naturally deprioritized.
exercises.explain: The whole point of blending recency into scoring is to make old, outdated content rank lower automatically, without a human manually filtering it out.

exercises.prompt: Which of these three retrieval score explanations was most likely AI-generated (sounds like generic filler rather than a grounded, specific explanation)?

options.text: 'We combine the cosine similarity score with an exponential decay based on document age, so a document from today gets close to full weight while one from a year ago gets scaled down significantly.'
'This system leverages cutting-edge synergy between relevance and recency to holistically empower optimal information retrieval outcomes for all users.'
'We tested three decay rates and found that halving the score every 30 days worked best for our news dataset, based on user click feedback.'

exercises.explain: Option B is full of vague buzzwords ('leverages,' 'synergy,' 'holistically empower') with no concrete mechanism or numbers — a classic sign of AI-generated filler text, unlike the specific, testable details in the other two.

exercises.prompt: Your system retrieves chunks for the query 'current CEO of Acme Corp.' Chunk A is a highly on-topic press release from 2 days ago naming a new CEO. Chunk B is an even more detailed, topically perfect bio page about the PREVIOUS CEO, unchanged for 3 years. Which combination of relevance + recency scoring gives the best final ranking?

options.text: Rank purely by topical match score, ignoring dates, so Chunk B wins on detail
Rank purely by newest timestamp, ignoring topic match, so any recent unrelated doc could outrank both
Combine topical relevance and a recency boost/decay so Chunk A's fresh, on-topic info outranks Chunk B's stale, on-topic info
Discard all documents older than a week regardless of content

exercises.explain: The goal is blending, not choosing one signal alone. Chunk B is topically strong but stale, so a pure relevance score would wrongly rank it high. Chunk A is both on-topic AND fresh, so a combined score (relevance times a recency weight/decay) correctly pushes it to the top while still allowing older docs to matter somewhat if nothing fresher exists. This blended scoring is the core idea of recency-weighted relevance, and it's a key building block for building retrieval systems that don't hand users outdated answers. You've now completed this phase of your LLM Application Engineering journey!
